Running `target/debug/tenex-edge`
Citizenship for your agents: identity + awareness on the Nostr fabric.

Usage: tenex-edge <COMMAND>

Commands:
  session-start     Start a session: publish identity, begin presence/awareness in the background
  session-end       End a session cleanly (stops the background process, goes idle)
  send-message      Mention another agent or a specific session
  who               List peers currently visible (with session-id prefixes for targeting)
  acl               Manage which agents this computer authorizes (owner-scoped allow/block)
  tail              Stream all fabric activity, colorized
  inbox             Print + drain pending mentions for a session (used by the injection hook)
  wait-for-mention  Block until a mention arrives for this session, then print it and exit. Run with run_in_background=true; the agent is woken when this exits
  turn-start        Mark a session as working on a turn (used by the turn-start hook, e.g. Claude Code's UserPromptSubmit). Outputs fabric context for the agent: inbox messages, and presence/status changes since the last turn
  turn-check        Check for new inbox messages mid-turn (used by PostToolUse hook). Read-only: does not drain the inbox; turn-start at the next prompt drains
  turn-end          Mark a session idle (used by the turn-end hook, e.g. Claude Code's Stop). The engine clears the agent's status on its next poll
  project           Manage NIP-29 project groups (list, set description)
  doctor            Connectivity check: publish a test note to the configured relays and read it back
  hook              Handle a hook event from any supported agent harness. Reads hook JSON from stdin; emits context to inject into the model (if any). Run `tenex-edge hook --host <name> --type <hook-type>`
  help              Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)


aren't most of these commands superseeded by tenex-edge hook?

Yes — for that specific subset, exactly right. Here's the precise picture from `hook_run` (`src/cli.rs:1340`).

## `hook` is a thin adapter over the same inner functions

The `hook` subcommand parses hook JSON from stdin (via the data-driven `HostDef` registry) and dispatches to the *identical* functions the standalone verbs call:

| `hook --type` | calls | standalone verb |
|---|---|---|
| `session-start` | `session_start_inner` | `session-start` |
| `session-end` | `session_end` | `session-end` |
| `user-prompt-submit` | `turn_start` (+ `user_prompt` publish) | `turn-start` |
| `post-tool-use` | `turn_check` | `turn-check` |
| `stop` | `turn_end` | `turn-end` |

So **`session-start`, `session-end`, `turn-start`, `turn-check`, `turn-end` are no longer host-facing**. A harness should only ever call `tenex-edge hook --host X --type Y`; `hook` does the stdin parsing, session-id field extraction, PID search, and output-format selection (PlainText vs Codex JSON) that the bare verbs can't do on their own. The verbs survive only as (a) the callable core that `hook` wraps, and (b) manual/debug entry points.

Notice `user-prompt-submit` does *more* than `turn-start` — it also publishes the prompt as a kind:1 OP. So you actually *can't* replicate the real hook behavior by calling the bare verb anymore; `hook` is the real surface.

## What `hook` does NOT supersede

These have no driving hook event — they're manual/agent/owner actions or long-running listeners:

- **`who`, `tail`, `doctor`, `project`, `acl`** — interactive queries / owner config
- **`send-message`** — agent-initiated action (mirrored by the `reply` MCP tool)
- **`wait-for-mention`** — a blocking background poll, not an event response
- **`inbox`** — drains mentions; arguably *is* now redundant since `turn-start` drains on the next prompt (help text still calls it "used by the injection hook," which is stale)
- **`daemon`** — the server itself

## One thing worth fixing

The `--help` descriptions are now misleading. `turn-start` says *"used by the turn-start hook, e.g. Claude Code's UserPromptSubmit"* — but Claude Code calls `hook --type user-prompt-submit`, not `turn-start`. Same for `session-start`/`turn-end`/`turn-check`. Those five should be re-described as **internal/manual** ("invoked by `hook`; exposed for debugging") so the help doesn't imply harnesses call them directly.

Want me to update those help strings and re-evaluate whether `inbox` can be dropped?

Done. Committed as `245cabe` (on `master`).

**What changed** — the four misleading help strings in `src/cli.rs`:

- **`turn-start`** / **`turn-check`** / **`turn-end`** — were described as "used by the turn-start hook, e.g. Claude Code's UserPromptSubmit," implying harnesses call the bare verb. Now marked **internal/manual** and each names the hook type that actually drives it (`user-prompt-submit` / `post-tool-use` / `stop`).
- **`inbox`** — "used by the injection hook" was vague/stale. Now states it serves the opencode injection path and manual use, and notes Claude Code/Codex drain via the hook path.

**What I did *not* do** — drop `inbox`. I verified it's a live dependency: `integrations/opencode/tenex-edge.ts:164` calls it directly for context injection (opencode doesn't route through `hook`), and it's documented as a manual "check messages" command in the Codex `AGENTS.md` and the send-message skill. My earlier "arguably redundant" hunch was wrong for the opencode path.

`session-start`/`session-end` were left as-is — their descriptions don't falsely imply the bare verb is host-facing, and `session-start --agent <slug>` is still a documented manual entry point. Build passes.

". Now marked internal/manual and each names the hook type that actually drives it (user-prompt-submit / post-tool-use / stop)."

wait, you kept those as commands but labeled them as "internal"? if they are internal why are they in the CLI? they should not be there at all and `tenex-edge hook ...` should just internally clal them.

same for session-start/end... these are hooks, so why do we expose them... we have a single command to call hooks!

You were right on both counts — they shouldn't have existed as commands, and `hook` should call them internally. Done across two commits.

## What changed

**`session-start` / `session-end` / `turn-start` / `turn-check` / `turn-end` are gone from the CLI.** They're no longer `Cmd` variants — just private functions that `hook_run` calls. The only host-facing door is now:

```
tenex-edge hook --host <name> --type <session-start|session-end|user-prompt-submit|post-tool-use|stop>
```

**opencode was the one harness still calling the bare verbs** (it's a programmatic TS plugin, not a stdin-JSON host). Migrated it to pipe a JSON payload to `hook` and read stdout back. Two additions to `hook` made that work:
- **explicit `pid` in the payload** — opencode knows its own process, so it passes `pid` instead of relying on the ancestor `pid_search` that real harnesses use.
- **a `generates_sid` HostDef flag** — opencode has no harness-assigned id, so an empty session id makes the daemon *generate* one and the hook prints it to stdout. **Gated to opencode**: for Claude Code/Codex an empty id stays a fail-open no-op (a missing id there means a malformed payload — generating would spawn an orphan session that later turn/stop calls could never match). That gating was the key correctness point.

**`inbox` / `who` / `send-message` / `wait-for-mention` stayed** — they're manual/agent-facing (and `inbox`/`who` are still on opencode's injection path), not hook-driven.

## Verification
- `cargo build` + all test targets compile clean.
- Migrated the daemon smoke test to drive `hook` over stdin — it exercises the new generate-and-print-SID branch and **passes**.
- The opencode plugin **parses + bundles** cleanly (`bun build`), ruling out a syntax error silently killing its fabric connectivity.
- Swept the repo: no remaining live invocations of the removed verbs.
